title: Penicillin Allergy Delabeling
objectives: Recognize the low positive predictive value of penicillin allergy labels
Risk-stratify patients based on allergy history
Apply appropriate testing and challenge protocols

sections.title: The Problem with Penicillin Allergy Labels
sections.content:
**Epidemiology:**
• 10% of US patients labeled "penicillin allergic"
• Only 1-10% of labeled patients are truly allergic when tested
• 80% of IgE-mediated penicillin allergy wanes within 10 years

**Why This Matters:**
• Patients receive broader-spectrum, more toxic antibiotics
• Increased Clostridioides difficile infections
• Higher healthcare costs
• Increased antibiotic resistance
• Worse surgical outcomes (SSI)

**Goal of Delabeling:**
Safely identify patients who can tolerate penicillins, allowing optimal antibiotic selection.

sections.title: Risk Stratification by History
sections.content:
**High-Risk (True Allergy More Likely):**
• Anaphylaxis within 1 hour
• Documented positive skin test
• Recurrent reactions to multiple penicillins
• Reaction to very low dose
• Recent reaction (<10 years ago)

**Low-Risk (Allergy Unlikely):**
• Reaction >10 years ago
• Unknown or vague reaction
• Family history only
• Isolated GI symptoms (nausea, diarrhea)
• Headache, fatigue
• Non-urticarial, non-pruritic rash

**Moderate-Risk:**
• Urticaria without other features
• Childhood reaction with limited details
• Pruritus alone

sections.title: Type of Reactions
sections.content:
**IgE-Mediated (Type I):**
• Onset: Minutes to 1 hour
• Symptoms: Urticaria, angioedema, bronchospasm, anaphylaxis
• Risk: Immediate reactions on re-exposure
• Testing: Skin testing effective

**T-Cell Mediated (Type IV):**
• Onset: Days after starting
• Includes: Maculopapular rash, fixed drug eruption
• Lower risk on re-exposure (may tolerate)

**Severe Cutaneous Reactions (SCAR):**
• SJS/TEN, DRESS, AGEP
• These are CONTRAINDICATIONS to rechallenge
• NO testing or challenge—permanent avoidance

**Non-Allergic:**
• GI upset, headache, yeast infection
• Not immune-mediated
• Safe to rechallenge

sections.title: Penicillin Skin Testing
sections.content:
**Components:**
• Major determinant: Penicilloyl-polylysine (Pre-Pen)
• Minor determinant: Penicillin G, penicilloate, penilloate
• Amoxicillin (if aminopenicillin-specific reaction suspected)

**Procedure:**
1. Prick/puncture testing first
2. If negative → Intradermal testing
3. Read at 15-20 minutes
4. Positive = wheal ≥3mm greater than saline control

**Negative Predictive Value:**
• >95% when using both major and minor determinants
• Patients with negative skin tests can safely receive penicillin

**When to Test:**
• History of IgE-mediated reaction
• Unknown reaction type needing clarification
• Moderate-risk history

**When NOT to Test:**
• History of SCAR (SJS, TEN, DRESS)
• Positive skin test does not rule out tolerance (false positives occur)
• Very low-risk history → can proceed directly to challenge

sections.title: Direct Oral Challenge
sections.content:
**For Low-Risk Patients:**
Can skip skin testing and proceed directly to oral challenge.

**Protocol (Example):**
1. Informed consent
2. Amoxicillin 250mg orally
3. Observe 1 hour
4. If no reaction → Full therapeutic dose
5. Observe additional 1 hour
6. If tolerated → Remove allergy label

**Setting:**
• Can be done in outpatient clinic for low-risk patients
• Higher-risk patients → allergist office with monitoring
• Emergency equipment available

**Success Rate:**
• >95% of low-risk patients tolerate challenge
• Even among those with positive history, majority tolerate

sections.title: Cross-Reactivity Between Beta-Lactams
sections.content:
**Penicillins and Cephalosporins:**
• Historical teaching: 10% cross-reactivity
• Actual rate: 1-2% (similar side chains)
• Lower generations have more similar side chains

**R-Group (Side Chain) Cross-Reactivity:**
Higher risk when side chains match:
• Amoxicillin ↔ Cefadroxil, Cefprozil
• Ampicillin ↔ Cefaclor, Cephalexin

**Safe Alternatives (Different Side Chains):**
• Ceftriaxone, cefotaxime, ceftazidime, cefepime
• Generally safe in penicillin-allergic patients

**Carbapenems:**
• <1% cross-reactivity with penicillins
• Can usually be given to penicillin-allergic patients

**Aztreonam:**
• No cross-reactivity with penicillins
• Safe in penicillin allergy
• BUT cross-reacts with ceftazidime (same side chain)

sections.title: Delabeling Protocols
sections.content:
**Electronic Health Record Integration:**
• Flag low-risk allergies for pharmacist review
• Decision support tools
• Automatic identification of candidates

**Pre-Operative Delabeling:**
• Surgical patients benefit most (cefazolin for SSI prophylaxis)
• Can test/challenge day of surgery or preoperatively

**Inpatient Delabeling:**
• Identify patients on broad-spectrum antibiotics
• If history is low-risk → oral challenge
• Document results clearly in EHR

**Steps After Successful Challenge:**
1. Document negative challenge in allergy section
2. Change label to "Penicillin allergy - NEGATIVE CHALLENGE"
3. Educate patient
4. Provide documentation for patient to carry
5. Consider prescribing amoxicillin to reinforce tolerance

sections.title: Special Populations
sections.content:
**Pregnant Patients:**
• Penicillin is first-line for GBS prophylaxis and syphilis
• Can skin test and desensitize if needed
• Challenge protocols similar to non-pregnant

**Pediatric Patients:**
• Most childhood "allergy" labels are viral exanthems
• Very low-risk population for true allergy
• Direct challenge often appropriate

**Critically Ill Patients:**
• If infection requires penicillin and patient is labeled allergic:
  - If reaction was mild → may give with monitoring
  - If reaction was severe → desensitization protocol

**Desensitization:**
• For patients with confirmed IgE allergy who NEED penicillin
• Temporary tolerance (must maintain continuous dosing)
• Performed in ICU or closely monitored setting
• Does not remove allergy—tolerance lost after treatment course

keyTakeaways: 90% of patients labeled penicillin-allergic can safely tolerate penicillins
Risk stratification by history guides testing vs. direct challenge
Low-risk patients can proceed directly to oral amoxicillin challenge
SCAR reactions (SJS, TEN, DRESS) are permanent contraindications—no testing
Cross-reactivity is based on side chain similarity, not beta-lactam ring
Successful delabeling improves antibiotic stewardship and patient outcomes

references.citation: Shenoy ES, Macy E, Rowe T, Blumenthal KG. Evaluation and Management of Penicillin Allergy: A Review. JAMA. 2019;321(2):188-199
references.keyContent: Comprehensive JAMA review on risk stratification and delabeling protocols

references.citation: Joint Task Force on Practice Parameters. Drug Allergy: An Updated Practice Parameter. Ann Allergy Asthma Immunol. 2010;105(4):259-273
references.keyContent: AAAAI/ACAAI practice parameters for drug allergy evaluation

references.citation: Macy E, Contreras R. Health Care Use and Serious Infection Prevalence Associated With Penicillin Allergy. Ann Allergy Asthma Immunol. 2014;112(3):246-252
references.keyContent: Evidence for adverse outcomes from penicillin allergy labels

references.citation: Blumenthal KG, Peter JG, Trubiano JA, Phillips EJ. Antibiotic Allergy. Lancet. 2019;393(10167):183-198

reviewQuestions.question: A 45-year-old reports a "rash" to amoxicillin 20 years ago as a child. She doesn't recall details. She needs antibiotic prophylaxis before dental work. What is the most appropriate approach?
options: Penicillin skin testing before any beta-lactam
reviewQuestions.explanation: This is a LOW-RISK history: vague childhood rash >10 years ago. Direct oral challenge with amoxicillin is appropriate without skin testing. Over 95% of such patients tolerate the challenge.

reviewQuestions.question: A patient with documented anaphylaxis to penicillin needs treatment for neurosyphilis. What is the appropriate management?
reviewQuestions.explanation: For neurosyphilis, penicillin is the only effective treatment. With confirmed severe IgE-mediated allergy, desensitization (not challenge) is required. This induces temporary tolerance while maintaining continuous dosing.

reviewQuestions.question: Which cephalosporin has the highest cross-reactivity risk with amoxicillin?
reviewQuestions.explanation: Cefadroxil shares the same R-group side chain as amoxicillin, giving it higher cross-reactivity risk. Ceftriaxone, cefepime, and ceftazidime have different side chains and lower cross-reactivity.
